APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—non‑projective dependency parsing with transition systems and dynamic oracles—and evaluates on standard CoNLL-X/XI benchmarks, so it is broadly within scope for a top venue. However, from my theoretical-purist perspective the work relies on an approximate dynamic oracle without formal error bounds and concedes expensive exact computations (cycle enumeration), which raises concerns about theoretical guarantees that top-tier conferences often expect; nonetheless the algorithmic focus, empirical improvements over relevant baselines, and thorough related work make it a good fit overall with minor scope concerns. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (transition-based non-projective dependency parsing) and proposes a practical, efficient approximation (non-monotonic Covington + approximate dynamic oracle) that is of clear interest to the venue. Experimental evaluation is strong in breadth (nine CoNLL-X and all CoNLL-XI datasets) and compares to appropriate monotonic and dynamic baselines, which matches the empirical robustness I expect. Minor concerns are the approximate oracle limitations, computational caveats (cycle enumeration capped) and absence of mentioned code/hyperparameter details, which reduce reproducibility but do not remove topical fit.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—non‑projective dependency parsing with transition systems and dynamic oracles—and evaluates on standard CoNLL-X/XI benchmarks, so it is broadly within scope for a top venue. However, from my theoretical-purist perspective the work relies on an approximate dynamic oracle without formal error bounds and concedes expensive exact computations (cycle enumeration), which raises concerns about theoretical guarantees that top-tier conferences often expect; nonetheless the algorithmic focus, empirical improvements over relevant baselines, and thorough related work make it a good fit overall with minor scope concerns.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (transition-based non-projective dependency parsing) and proposes a practical, efficient approximation (non-monotonic Covington + approximate dynamic oracle) that is of clear interest to the venue. Experimental evaluation is strong in breadth (nine CoNLL-X and all CoNLL-XI datasets) and compares to appropriate monotonic and dynamic baselines, which matches the empirical robustness I expect. Minor concerns are the approximate oracle limitations, computational caveats (cycle enumeration capped) and absence of mentioned code/hyperparameter details, which reduce reproducibility but do not remove topical fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser against appropriate baselines (static oracle, monotonic dynamic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González 2015) on standard CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets using the same accuracy metric, which supports fair empirical comparison. It explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods are competitive and analyzes differences between monotonic and non‑monotonic variants; related work coverage is reported as thorough. As a theoretical reviewer I note no evidence in the summary of comparisons to a broader set of recent strong non‑transition or graph‑based parsers (minor omission) and the approximate oracle’s implications are not bounded, so I deduct one point for these comparative/theoretical gaps. | B(4): The paper evaluates the proposed non‑monotonic Covington parser against the most direct and relevant baselines (static oracle, dynamic monotonic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez and Fernández‑González 2015) across many CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI corpora using the same accuracy metric—this is empirically strong and aligns with my pragmatic priorities. However, comparisons appear narrowly focused on variants of the same transition system and do not report broader state‑of‑the‑art or alternative parsing paradigms (e.g., other recent non‑monotonic or strong graph‑based baselines), and experimental details (code/hyperparameters) are not mentioned, so I mark a minor omission rather than a fatal one.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed non-monotonic Covington parser against appropriate baselines (static oracle, monotonic dynamic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González 2015) on standard CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets using the same accuracy metric, which supports fair empirical comparison. It explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods are competitive and analyzes differences between monotonic and non‑monotonic variants; related work coverage is reported as thorough. As a theoretical reviewer I note no evidence in the summary of comparisons to a broader set of recent strong non‑transition or graph‑based parsers (minor omission) and the approximate oracle’s implications are not bounded, so I deduct one point for these comparative/theoretical gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed non‑monotonic Covington parser against the most direct and relevant baselines (static oracle, dynamic monotonic oracle, and the monotonic Covington parser of Gómez‑Rodríguez and Fernández‑González 2015) across many CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI corpora using the same accuracy metric—this is empirically strong and aligns with my pragmatic priorities. However, comparisons appear narrowly focused on variants of the same transition system and do not report broader state‑of‑the‑art or alternative parsing paradigms (e.g., other recent non‑monotonic or strong graph‑based baselines), and experimental details (code/hyperparameters) are not mentioned, so I mark a minor omission rather than a fatal one.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel contribution: a fully non-monotonic variant of the non-projective Covington transition system that allows Right-Arc and Left-Arc transitions to overwrite previous heads and repair past errors—this is a clear methodological departure from the earlier monotonic Covington parser. It also introduces an approximate dynamic oracle tailored to the non-monotonic, non-projective setting using efficiently computable loss approximations, which is non-trivial given the lack of arc-decomposability and the need to avoid costly exact cycle enumeration. While the work builds on existing Covington-based and dynamic-oracle frameworks (so it is not a wholly new problem), the specific combination and the new transition behavior represent a meaningful and informative extension that would interest experts. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new combination: a fully non-monotonic variant of the non-projective Covington transition system together with an efficiently-computable approximate dynamic oracle, which goes beyond the prior monotonic Covington work (Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González, 2015). The need to handle non-monotonic overwriting of heads and to approximate loss due to non-arc-decomposability makes the technical approach a non-trivial extension rather than a straightforward application of existing methods. The empirical demonstration across CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets further differentiates it from prior monotonic baselines. Given these points, the contribution is a notable novel advance though not a radical paradigm shift, hence not the topmost score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel contribution: a fully non-monotonic variant of the non-projective Covington transition system that allows Right-Arc and Left-Arc transitions to overwrite previous heads and repair past errors—this is a clear methodological departure from the earlier monotonic Covington parser. It also introduces an approximate dynamic oracle tailored to the non-monotonic, non-projective setting using efficiently computable loss approximations, which is non-trivial given the lack of arc-decomposability and the need to avoid costly exact cycle enumeration. While the work builds on existing Covington-based and dynamic-oracle frameworks (so it is not a wholly new problem), the specific combination and the new transition behavior represent a meaningful and informative extension that would interest experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new combination: a fully non-monotonic variant of the non-projective Covington transition system together with an efficiently-computable approximate dynamic oracle, which goes beyond the prior monotonic Covington work (Gómez‑Rodríguez & Fernández‑González, 2015). The need to handle non-monotonic overwriting of heads and to approximate loss due to non-arc-decomposability makes the technical approach a non-trivial extension rather than a straightforward application of existing methods. The empirical demonstration across CoNLL‑X and CoNLL‑XI datasets further differentiates it from prior monotonic baselines. Given these points, the contribution is a notable novel advance though not a radical paradigm shift, hence not the topmost score.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions (non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. Notation and presentation are generally clear and writing quality is reported as good; figures/tables and examples are said to be effective and related work coverage thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: experimental details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the paper could more clearly justify/clarify the practical consequences of the stated limitation (e.g. the 100,000-transition analysis cap). From a formalist perspective these omissions are presentation-level concerns rather than failures of exposition. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized with clear early statement of contributions and a readable method->experiments->conclusion flow; notation and definitions appear consistent and related work is thoroughly covered. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, making the technical ideas understandable. I dock one point because experimental reporting omits hyperparameter details and code availability, which hampers clarity and reproducibility of the empirical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions (non-monotonic Covington system and an approximate dynamic oracle) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. Notation and presentation are generally clear and writing quality is reported as good; figures/tables and examples are said to be effective and related work coverage thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: experimental details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and the paper could more clearly justify/clarify the practical consequences of the stated limitation (e.g. the 100,000-transition analysis cap). From a formalist perspective these omissions are presentation-level concerns rather than failures of exposition.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized with clear early statement of contributions and a readable method->experiments->conclusion flow; notation and definitions appear consistent and related work is thoroughly covered. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and writing quality is good, making the technical ideas understandable. I dock one point because experimental reporting omits hyperparameter details and code availability, which hampers clarity and reproducibility of the empirical claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results are strong and the related work/limitations are well discussed, but they also concur that missing hyperparameter/code release, lack of ablation/sensitivity analyses, and absence of formal guarantees for the approximate dynamic oracle are important shortcomings. Given these reproducibility and theoretical gaps—particularly the unquantified approximation error and computational limits—I assign a slightly conservative score of 3 to reflect solid empirical contribution tempered by insufficient analysis and guarantees.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate to show empirical gains across CoNLL-X / XI datasets, but it lacks ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g., hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and is constrained by expensive cycle computations (analysis capped at 100k transitions). The paper acknowledges the approximate dynamic oracle and computational limits but provides no formal bounds on the oracle's approximation error or worst-case complexity of the non-monotonic loss computation; from a theoretical standpoint this is a significant omission. Related work appears well covered and limitations are discussed, but reproducibility and theoretical guarantees are insufficient for a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in coverage: the authors test on all CoNLL-XI datasets and nine CoNLL-X corpora and compare against both static and the monotonic Covington dynamic oracle baseline, demonstrating consistent gains. However, there is no mention of released code or reported hyperparameters, and the approximate dynamic-oracle design is not accompanied by ablation or sensitivity analyses quantifying the impact of the approximations (e.g. the effect of truncated cycle analysis), which hurts reproducibility and understanding of failure modes. The paper does acknowledge important limitations of the approximate loss and computational costs, and related work appears thoroughly covered. Overall this is a solid empirical contribution with minor but important gaps in reproducibility and analysis depth.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and demonstrates consistent empirical gains with a fully non-monotonic Covington system and an efficient approximate dynamic oracle across many languages. They also agree limitations remain—reliance on an approximate oracle without formal bounds, computational costs for some analyses, and limited reproducibility details—which temper the theoretical impact. Given the clear practical benefits and likely influence on practitioners despite these caveats, a slightly above-moderate impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—training non-projective, fully non-monotonic transition-based parsers—and proposes a concrete system (non-monotonic Covington) plus an efficient approximate dynamic oracle that yields empirical gains over the monotonic baseline on CoNLL-X/XI datasets. However, from a theoretical-perspective the reliance on an approximate oracle without bounds and the admitted computational costs (e.g., needing to approximate cycle-related loss and limiting analysis to 100k transitions) reduce the likelihood of this becoming a definitive methodological shift. The work is nonetheless useful: it demonstrates practical benefit and opens follow-up research directions on providing formal guarantees and tighter complexity/approximation analyses, so I assess its impact as moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—allowing error repair in non-projective transition parsing—and provides an empirically robust solution evaluated across the broad CoNLL-X / CoNLL-XI suite (multiple languages), which is the kind of empirical evidence I value. The proposed fully non-monotonic Covington system plus an efficiently-computable approximate dynamic oracle makes previously intractable training feasible and yields consistent gains over the monotonic baseline on a majority of datasets, so it will change how practitioners approach robustness in transition parsers. That said, reliance on an approximate oracle (and expensive cycle computations for some analyses), plus no mention of released code or detailed hyperparameters, limits immediate reproducibility and wider adoption; these caveats keep the contribution from being fully transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear algorithmic and empirical contribution but lacks formal guarantees (soundness/completeness proofs), exact-loss computation, and thorough analysis of the approximate oracle and complexity. While experiments show improvements and the approach is plausible, missing analytical bounds, reproducibility details (hyperparameters/code) and limited investigation of approximation errors justify a cautious score reflecting partial technical support but unresolved methodological gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear algorithmic contribution (a fully non-monotonic Covington variant) and empirical gains, but from the provided summary it lacks formal guarantees: I see no proofs of soundness/completeness or invariants for the non-monotonic transitions, nor any bounds on the approximation error of the proposed dynamic oracle. The authors acknowledge computational obstacles (cycle enumeration) and limit analyses, yet do not supply worst-case or amortized complexity results for the system or the oracle, which is a serious omission for a parsing-transition proposal. Empirically the comparisons appear reasonable, but missing hyperparameter details and the approximate nature of the oracle mean the technical claims are only partially supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental evidence showing the non-monotonic dynamic oracle/parser improves over the monotonic Covington baseline on a majority of CoNLL-X/XI datasets, and it acknowledges the approximate nature of the oracle and computational limits (cycles, 100k transition cap). The mathematical treatment appears coherent for a non-arc-decomposable system and the approximation strategy is plausible, but the lack of exact-loss computation, missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code hinder full reproducibility and raise practical concerns. Overall the methodology and comparisons to relevant baselines are sound, though some empirical and analytical gaps (e.g., statistical significance reporting, more thorough exploration of the approximation errors and settings) prevent a fully flawless rating.

main_claims: Proposes a fully non-monotonic transition system based on the non-projective Covington parser that allows Right-Arc and Left-Arc transitions to overwrite previous heads and to repair past errors.
Introduces an approximate dynamic oracle for training the non-monotonic parser, using efficiently-computable approximations of loss in the non-monotonic non-projective setting.

Demonstrates experimentally that the non-monotonic dynamic oracle/parser outperforms the monotonic version (Gómez-Rodríguez and Fernández-González, 2015) on a majority of tested datasets from the CoNLL-X and CoNLL-XI shared tasks.
_summary.methodology: Define a non-monotonic variant of the non-projective Covington transition system where arc transitions always allowed and conflicting arcs are removed to enforce single-head and acyclicity; train the parser with a dynamic oracle that approximates loss efficiently (avoiding exact, costly computations).
limitations: The dynamic oracle is approximate due to the complexity of computing exact loss in a non-monotonic, non-projective setting.
Computing certain non-monotonic loss terms requires finding all elementary cycles, which is computationally expensive, so analysis was limited to the first 100,000 transitions per dataset.
The non-projective Covington parser is not arc-decomposable, complicating exact loss computation.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - non-projective dependency parsing / transition-based parsing

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics and algorithms with a strong record of formal analyses of parsing algorithms and dynamic oracles.
traits: penalises papers that introduce algorithmic changes without formal correctness proofs (e.g., soundness/completeness or clear invariants)
penalises papers that propose approximate training objectives/oracles without bounds on approximation error or worst-case behavior
requires explicit complexity/runtime analysis (worst-case and amortized) for new transition systems and for training/inference procedures
favors papers that provide formal guarantees or rigorous theoretical arguments even if empirical evaluation is limited
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal properties and guarantees, demanding proofs or tight theoretical analysis for algorithmic claims and treating empirical gains as secondary support.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP/ML researcher and practitioner who develops and evaluates parsing systems for real-world and multilingual corpora, with emphasis on robust, efficient methods.
traits: rewards papers that show consistent empirical improvements across multiple standard datasets and languages (prefers results on >=5 corpora)
rewards proposals of efficient approximations that make previously intractable training or inference practical
penalises papers lacking thorough empirical baselines (expects comparison to closest monotonic and non-monotonic baselines and to state-of-the-art where applicable)
values reproducibility: expects code release or sufficiently detailed experimental setup to allow replication
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes empirical robustness and practical impact, favoring methods that produce consistent gains and enable efficient training/inference even if some theoretical aspects are approximate.
